The PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler delivers precise temperature gradient control, flexible workflows, and productivity features to help labs achieve reliable results and work more efficiently. Share PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler: Leading PCR performance and productivity for modern labs The PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler is an advanced PCR thermal cycler designed for researchers who require precise, reproducible DNA amplification and flexible workflow capabilities. For laboratories evaluating the best PCR thermal cycler, PowerFlex combines high thermal accuracy, fast ramp rates, and multi-format compatibility to support a wide range of molecular biology applications. Built on decades of PCR innovation, the Applied Biosystems™ PowerFlex Thermal Cycler delivers consistent thermal cycling performance, supports multiple experiment types, and improves lab efficiency through intuitive controls and remote connectivity. Performance: High-precision temperature gradient control for reliable results Achieving consistent, high-quality PCR results starts with precise temperature control. The PowerFlex Thermal Cycler features advanced VeriFlex™ block technology, delivering thermal accuracy of ±0.15°C and uniformity of 0.4°C across wells. Fast ramp rates of up to 7°C per second reduce cycle times while maintaining amplification quality and reaction integrity, enabling higher throughput without compromising data quality. The optimized block design ensures uniform heating and cooling, reducing variability and improving reproducibility across experiments. This level of precision supports a wide range of applications—including standard PCR and assay optimization—and provides researchers with more reliable amplification, improved data consistency, and greater confidence in experimental outcomes. Compatibility: flexible configurations for diverse applications PowerFlex supports a wide range of PCR consumables—including non-skirted, semi-skirted, and fully skirted plates, as well as tubes and other plastics formats—helping modern laboratories adapt to evolving workflows. The system offers multiple block configurations, including 96-well and 3 × 32-well formats, allowing researchers to run different experiments simultaneously. Independent temperature zones enable optimization of multiple assays in a single run, reducing the need for additional instruments. This flexibility allows labs to standardize workflows while maintaining the ability to adapt to new protocols and experimental requirements. Productivity: Streamlined workflows Efficiency is essential in high-throughput and multi-user lab environments. PowerFlex features a redesigned user interface on a 10.1-inch touchscreen, delivering clear visibility and intuitive navigation for faster setup and experiment monitoring. Independent block temperature control enables multiple experiments or users to run concurrently, improving instrument utilization, while a compact footprint helps conserve valuable bench space. Integrated Smart Help functionality allows users to submit support requests directly from the instrument, eliminating the need to step away from the lab bench. Instrument data can be shared with the service team, at the user’s discretion, to enable more accurate troubleshooting—reducing downtime and saving valuable research time. Remote access through Thermo Fisher Connect lets users to upload methods, monitor runs, and manage experiments from a computer or mobile device. Delivering confidence in every PCR run For researchers seeking the best PCR thermal cycler, the PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler delivers a strong balance of precision, flexibility, and operational efficiency. Designed as a high-performance PCR system, it provides consistent, reproducible results while supporting diverse workflows across a range of applications. PowerFlex enables researchers to optimize experiments, improve throughput, and generate high-quality DNA amplification data with confidence—making it a valuable addition to any modern molecular biology laboratory. Weight 13 kg (28.7 lb) 15 kg (33 lb) PCR volume range 1–100 µL VeriFlex Blocks range 6 temperature zones (up to 10°C zone-to-zone), 30°C range across the block 2 temperature zones (up to 10°C zone-to-zone) Smart Help Yes Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, anyaivanova You might also be interested in... Interview Postdoc Portrait: Jessica Strosahl Working at the intersection of genetics and nutrition, this postdoc investigates protein metabolism in individuals with inherited metabolic disorders. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. It is read by leading researchers in industry and academia who value penetrating analyses and broad perspectives on life-science topics both within and beyond their areas of expertise. Written by prominent scientists and professional journalists, articles in are concise, accurate, accessible, and entertaining. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jan 12, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc focuses on addressing long-standing gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with rare genetic disorders. Image credit: Solanch Dupeyron, ©iStock.com, Liudmila Chernetska PDF version Share Q | What's your research background? My name is Jessica Strosahl , and I am a Postdoctoral Fellow and Genetic Metabolic Dietitian under the mentorship of Rani Singh in the Department of Human Genetics at Emory University. I work within the Medical Nutrition Therapy for Prevention Program, which addresses critical gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs) and other genetic disorders. Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? My passion for IMD research began during my undergraduate education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where I worked as a nutrition assistant at the Biochemical Genetics Clinic. In that role, I witnessed firsthand the physical and emotional challenges faced by individuals living with rare diseases. These experiences profoundly shaped my career goals and inspired me to pursue a PhD and obtain my Registered Dietitian Nutritionist credential. I aspired to bridge the gap between clinical care and metabolic research to improve the lives of patients with IMDs. I’m grateful that in my current role, I can bring that vision to life by conducting translational research that advances dietary treatments for individuals living with IMDs. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite projects is our interventional clinical trial investigating protein requirements in adults with PKU—a major evidence gap in the field. Current PKU protein recommendations are extrapolated from outdated nitrogen balance studies in the general population, which likely underestimate true needs. Our study uses the state-of-the-art, minimally invasive indicator amino acid oxidation method to directly measure protein requirements and stratify by participant genotype. Because individuals with PKU rely on varying amounts of phenylalanine-free medical protein supplements, we also aim to determine whether protein needs differ based on medical protein prescriptions. Participants attend 8-hour visits at Emory’s Clinical Research Center, consuming hourly meal shakes composed of amino acids, isotopic tracers, and carbohydrate–fat blends—topped off with a specially formulated cookie baked in our lab. This experience has taught me how challenging participant recruitment can be in rare disease research, and how essential it is to have strong mentorship and institutional support. I’m grateful to work with Singh and Emory University, who make this research possible. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career so far? Beginning my first clinical trial and receiving my first grant as principal investigator have been the most exciting and rewarding milestones of my scientific career so far. Both represent pivotal steps toward establishing my independence as a researcher and advancing evidence-based nutrition care in inherited metabolic disorders. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would be dual-energy X-ray absorptiometer (DXA) scanner—it reveals what’s beneath the surface to give a clearer, more complete picture. My research takes a similar approach, integrating genotype, metabolism, and diet to inform personalized nutrition strategies that better support individuals with IMDs. Article Using Site-Saturation Variant Libraries to Understand Viral Evolution Discover how scientists bridge the gap between genomic detection and functional assessment to remain a step ahead of viral evolution. Read More Additionally, he said that implementing a One Health or multidisciplinary approach could bring more experts to the table to improve surveillance and mitigation. “It is fundamentally a way to de-silo how we approach problems in science,” Cross said. “So instead of looking at it [as] a single basic science pathway towards one part of an answer, we try to develop an integrated set of ways to investigate a problem.” For example, as a coleader of the New World Screwworm response at Texas Tech University where he directs the team’s research efforts, he said that the group has brought in both veterinarians and physicians for educational opportunities. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9 The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico. Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest . Jan 12, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc focuses on addressing long-standing gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with rare genetic disorders. Image credit: Solanch Dupeyron, ©iStock.com, Liudmila Chernetska PDF version Share Q | What's your research background? My name is Jessica Strosahl , and I am a Postdoctoral Fellow and Genetic Metabolic Dietitian under the mentorship of Rani Singh in the Department of Human Genetics at Emory University. I work within the Medical Nutrition Therapy for Prevention Program, which addresses critical gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs) and other genetic disorders. Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? My passion for IMD research began during my undergraduate education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where I worked as a nutrition assistant at the Biochemical Genetics Clinic. In that role, I witnessed firsthand the physical and emotional challenges faced by individuals living with rare diseases. These experiences profoundly shaped my career goals and inspired me to pursue a PhD and obtain my Registered Dietitian Nutritionist credential. I aspired to bridge the gap between clinical care and metabolic research to improve the lives of patients with IMDs. I’m grateful that in my current role, I can bring that vision to life by conducting translational research that advances dietary treatments for individuals living with IMDs. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite projects is our interventional clinical trial investigating protein requirements in adults with PKU—a major evidence gap in the field. Current PKU protein recommendations are extrapolated from outdated nitrogen balance studies in the general population, which likely underestimate true needs. Our study uses the state-of-the-art, minimally invasive indicator amino acid oxidation method to directly measure protein requirements and stratify by participant genotype. Because individuals with PKU rely on varying amounts of phenylalanine-free medical protein supplements, we also aim to determine whether protein needs differ based on medical protein prescriptions. Participants attend 8-hour visits at Emory’s Clinical Research Center, consuming hourly meal shakes composed of amino acids, isotopic tracers, and carbohydrate–fat blends—topped off with a specially formulated cookie baked in our lab. This experience has taught me how challenging participant recruitment can be in rare disease research, and how essential it is to have strong mentorship and institutional support. I’m grateful to work with Singh and Emory University, who make this research possible. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career so far? Beginning my first clinical trial and receiving my first grant as principal investigator have been the most exciting and rewarding milestones of my scientific career so far. Both represent pivotal steps toward establishing my independence as a researcher and advancing evidence-based nutrition care in inherited metabolic disorders. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would be dual-energy X-ray absorptiometer (DXA) scanner—it reveals what’s beneath the surface to give a clearer, more complete picture. My research takes a similar approach, integrating genotype, metabolism, and diet to inform personalized nutrition strategies that better support individuals with IMDs. News As the New World Screwworm Returns, Scientists Unite to Fight Back As local and federal authorities respond to the current outbreak of the flesh-eating blowfly, researchers look ahead to new approaches to keep New World screwworm at bay. Written by Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby Bradford, PhD Shelby is an Assistant Editor for The Scientist . She earned her PhD from West Virginia University in immunology and microbiology and completed an AAAS Mass Media fellowship. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 19, 2026 | 6 min read Save for Later A blowfly that lays its eggs in wounds of live animals poses major health and safety risks. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Lidya Elfa Sari PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 6:00 Share I nsect pests like flies are a nuisance, but some, like the New World screwworm, have the potential to devastate cattle, a serious and costly bane to the agriculture industry. The New World screwworm is a type of blowfly that sniffs out and lays eggs in the wounds of living tissue, often livestock and companion animals. When the larvae emerge, they eat the surrounding flesh before falling out to mature in the soil, causing more injury that attracts more blowflies to the site in the meantime. Before mitigation efforts, New World screwworm led to more than $200 million in livestock losses every year in the US alone. 1 While strategies to eliminate New World screwworm populations have worked well in the past, today, researchers are exploring new genetic technologies and multidisciplinary approaches to better combat this devastating pest. Sterile Insects Kept New World Screwworm at Bay The New World screwworm is endemic to the warm regions of the Americas. To combat this devastating fly, scientists took advantage of the insect’s sexual behavior. 2-4 Male blowflies mate aggressively, while female blowflies only mate once. By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe “It was a brilliant idea,” said Maxwell Scott , an entomologist at North Carolina State University. Indeed, the US in coordination with Mexico and, later, central American countries drove the New World screwworm out of their territories down to Panama in the 1960s. There, a production facility has continued to churn out up to 100 million flies a week to maintain a barrier between Panama and Colombia. “So, it can be very effective, but it is a numbers game. You've got to outnumber the fertile flies in the area,” Scott said. However, in 2022, the barricade broke. Researchers said that it was likely a combination of many factors: Varying land use had introduced more cattle into the area, the COVID-19 pandemic caused a decrease in the number of people available to conduct investigations, and the number of flies released into the area wasn’t increased in sufficient time when early cases were detected. “We have to take these factors into account, and the long-term management will require coordinated international action rather than isolated local responses,” said Andrés Lira-Noriega , an ecologist studying the interaction between species, including New World screwworm, and their environment at the Institute of Ecology. The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said. On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico. While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved. One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Since these flies can lay eggs in both animal and human tissues, the team wants to make sure that the medical professionals are ready to spot and treat new cases of New World Screwworm. At the same time, they are working with environmental scientists to track cases and develop models to predict where these flies may be most likely to migrate to, which can inform where field personnel need to be sent with interventions. Lira-Noriega said that this is important because researchers cannot physically monitor every location, so predictive models help identify where resources are most needed to respond efficiently. For example, he and his team investigated the factors that influenced where New World Screwworm would likely migrate after the 2022 breach of the Panama barrier. 6 He said that, so far, the spread of the fly has matched his and his team’s predictions, but he added that they found many more areas that have suitable conditions for this fly. He thinks that surveillance and mitigation efforts should extend to these regions as well before cases emerge. “Historically the sterile insect technique has been really useful and is today probably one of the most promising approaches, but definitely it has to be combined with surveillance, rapid response, [and] regional cooperation,” he said. He added that integrating existing tools, such as geospatial information, reported cases, and the distribution of livestock can help public health officials monitor outbreaks in real time and prioritize responses. Lira-Noriega also said that incorporating newer molecular surveillance technologies could further improve surveillance. Cross and his team are working on just those types of solutions. They are collaborating with biochemists and engineers to develop better field tests to detect New World Screwworm, including lateral flow tests to detect the blowfly’s DNA from the wounds of infected animals and strategies to identify the presence of the fly from water trap samples using environmental DNA testing. Male Only Flies: The Next Evolution in the Sterile Insect Technique Another area that researchers have explored improving is in the sterile insect technique itself. Currently, when facilities produce flies, they raise both male and female insects because differentiating them on the scale of millions is not feasible. This leads to raising and releasing female blowflies that don’t largely aid in the elimination strategy, essentially wasting resources. “It's enormously expensive, so that's why people like myself have been searching for more efficient solutions,” Scott said. He and his team developed a male-only line of New World Screwworm flies to overcome this challenge. The researchers use a transgene under the control of tetracycline that is spliced with an intron so that a lethal protein is only overexpressed in female larvae and embryos. 7-9 The Environmental Protection Agency recently chose one of the team’s lines to undergo field testing in Panama. If successful, Scott’s fly line could be approved for use in the US, and with additional regulatory approval, in Mexico. Switching to male-only flies, Scott said, would allow facilities to produce and release more flies that are directly relevant to the mitigation strategy. Additionally, he said that since male flies can be sterilized more easily than female flies, the producers can use lower doses of radiation to perform that task. This reduction could help keep these flies fitter for mating. Looking further ahead, some researchers are exploring avoiding radiation all together. Instead, they hope to use CRISPR-Cas9 -based gene editing to render the flies sterile. Scott said that this strategy would be cheaper. However, researchers currently struggle to grow these colonies at scale, since the approach involves many genetic crosses. One of these strategies or another novel approach could soon get some additional help in coming to fruition. Earlier in the year, the USDA opened a grant application for, in part, novel strategies or enhancements of existing approaches with new technology to improve the control of the New World screwworm. “I suspect we'll find some really neat and inventive molecular techniques being posited as potential solutions to help with the issue,” Cross said. Today, researchers have the tools necessary to combat the New World screwworm, but by working together and implementing new strategies and better technology, scientists and other stakeholders can stay one step ahead of this deadly pest . I nsect pests like flies are a nuisance, but some, like the New World screwworm, have the potential to devastate cattle, a serious and costly bane to the agriculture industry. The New World screwworm is a type of blowfly that sniffs out and lays eggs in the wounds of living tissue, often livestock and companion animals. When the larvae emerge, they eat the surrounding flesh before falling out to mature in the soil, causing more injury that attracts more blowflies to the site in the meantime. Before mitigation efforts, New World screwworm led to more than $200 million in livestock losses every year in the US alone. 1 While strategies to eliminate New World screwworm populations have worked well in the past, today, researchers are exploring new genetic technologies and multidisciplinary approaches to better combat this devastating pest. Sterile Insects Kept New World Screwworm at Bay The New World screwworm is endemic to the warm regions of the Americas. To combat this devastating fly, scientists took advantage of the insect’s sexual behavior. 2-4 Male blowflies mate aggressively, while female blowflies only mate once. By sterilizing large numbers of flies to release them into the wild, researchers discovered that they could drive out the blowflies by causing large numbers of unsuccessful pairings. 5 Continue reading below... Sign up for FREE Immunology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe “It was a brilliant idea,” said Maxwell Scott , an entomologist at North Carolina State University. Indeed, the US in coordination with Mexico and, later, central American countries drove the New World screwworm out of their territories down to Panama in the 1960s. There, a production facility has continued to churn out up to 100 million flies a week to maintain a barrier between Panama and Colombia. “So, it can be very effective, but it is a numbers game. You've got to outnumber the fertile flies in the area,” Scott said. However, in 2022, the barricade broke. Researchers said that it was likely a combination of many factors: Varying land use had introduced more cattle into the area, the COVID-19 pandemic caused a decrease in the number of people available to conduct investigations, and the number of flies released into the area wasn’t increased in sufficient time when early cases were detected. “We have to take these factors into account, and the long-term management will require coordinated international action rather than isolated local responses,” said Andrés Lira-Noriega , an ecologist studying the interaction between species, including New World screwworm, and their environment at the Institute of Ecology. The blowfly gradually migrated north. “[Mexican officials have] done a great job slowing it down, but there's just not enough flies. One hundred million sounds like a lot, but when you've got to really outnumber the fertile males to stop the population from increasing, that's not enough,” Scott said. On June 3, 2026, the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) announced the first case of New World screwworm in the US in a young calf in Texas. Since then, USDA in collaboration with other agencies in both the US and Mexico have launched responses to track, identify, and mitigate cases of New World screwworm. A new facility to produce up to 300 million sterilized flies per week is under construction in Texas, and a different facility is being retrofitted for this task on a smaller scale in Mexico. While these rapid responses are a commendable example of cooperation and outbreak management, this incident has also provided researchers with an opportunity to consider how the current approaches could be improved. One Health Approaches Can Better Address Complex Problems Chad Cross , a One Health researcher at Texas Tech University, said that, while the sterile insect technique strategy is solid for managing the New World Screwworm, the current outbreak highlights some limitations in the approach. He said that relying on one facility to produce the necessary flies was less than ideal, and that even if it’s not feasible to constantly maintain multiple facilities, having procedures to rapidly increase production early in an outbreak could help. Jan 12, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later This postdoc focuses on addressing long-standing gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with rare genetic disorders. Image credit: Solanch Dupeyron, ©iStock.com, Liudmila Chernetska PDF version Share Q | What's your research background? My name is Jessica Strosahl , and I am a Postdoctoral Fellow and Genetic Metabolic Dietitian under the mentorship of Rani Singh in the Department of Human Genetics at Emory University. I work within the Medical Nutrition Therapy for Prevention Program, which addresses critical gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs) and other genetic disorders. Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? My passion for IMD research began during my undergraduate education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where I worked as a nutrition assistant at the Biochemical Genetics Clinic. In that role, I witnessed firsthand the physical and emotional challenges faced by individuals living with rare diseases. These experiences profoundly shaped my career goals and inspired me to pursue a PhD and obtain my Registered Dietitian Nutritionist credential. I aspired to bridge the gap between clinical care and metabolic research to improve the lives of patients with IMDs. I’m grateful that in my current role, I can bring that vision to life by conducting translational research that advances dietary treatments for individuals living with IMDs. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite projects is our interventional clinical trial investigating protein requirements in adults with PKU—a major evidence gap in the field. Current PKU protein recommendations are extrapolated from outdated nitrogen balance studies in the general population, which likely underestimate true needs. Our study uses the state-of-the-art, minimally invasive indicator amino acid oxidation method to directly measure protein requirements and stratify by participant genotype. Because individuals with PKU rely on varying amounts of phenylalanine-free medical protein supplements, we also aim to determine whether protein needs differ based on medical protein prescriptions. Participants attend 8-hour visits at Emory’s Clinical Research Center, consuming hourly meal shakes composed of amino acids, isotopic tracers, and carbohydrate–fat blends—topped off with a specially formulated cookie baked in our lab. This experience has taught me how challenging participant recruitment can be in rare disease research, and how essential it is to have strong mentorship and institutional support. I’m grateful to work with Singh and Emory University, who make this research possible. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career so far? Beginning my first clinical trial and receiving my first grant as principal investigator have been the most exciting and rewarding milestones of my scientific career so far. Both represent pivotal steps toward establishing my independence as a researcher and advancing evidence-based nutrition care in inherited metabolic disorders. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would be dual-energy X-ray absorptiometer (DXA) scanner—it reveals what’s beneath the surface to give a clearer, more complete picture. My research takes a similar approach, integrating genotype, metabolism, and diet to inform personalized nutrition strategies that better support individuals with IMDs. My name is Jessica Strosahl , and I am a Postdoctoral Fellow and Genetic Metabolic Dietitian under the mentorship of Rani Singh in the Department of Human Genetics at Emory University. I work within the Medical Nutrition Therapy for Prevention Program, which addresses critical gaps in research, education, and patient care for individuals with inherited metabolic disorders (IMDs) and other genetic disorders. Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? My passion for IMD research began during my undergraduate education at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where I worked as a nutrition assistant at the Biochemical Genetics Clinic. In that role, I witnessed firsthand the physical and emotional challenges faced by individuals living with rare diseases. These experiences profoundly shaped my career goals and inspired me to pursue a PhD and obtain my Registered Dietitian Nutritionist credential. I aspired to bridge the gap between clinical care and metabolic research to improve the lives of patients with IMDs. I’m grateful that in my current role, I can bring that vision to life by conducting translational research that advances dietary treatments for individuals living with IMDs. Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite projects is our interventional clinical trial investigating protein requirements in adults with PKU—a major evidence gap in the field. Current PKU protein recommendations are extrapolated from outdated nitrogen balance studies in the general population, which likely underestimate true needs. Our study uses the state-of-the-art, minimally invasive indicator amino acid oxidation method to directly measure protein requirements and stratify by participant genotype. Because individuals with PKU rely on varying amounts of phenylalanine-free medical protein supplements, we also aim to determine whether protein needs differ based on medical protein prescriptions. Participants attend 8-hour visits at Emory’s Clinical Research Center, consuming hourly meal shakes composed of amino acids, isotopic tracers, and carbohydrate–fat blends—topped off with a specially formulated cookie baked in our lab. This experience has taught me how challenging participant recruitment can be in rare disease research, and how essential it is to have strong mentorship and institutional support. I’m grateful to work with Singh and Emory University, who make this research possible. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Genetics updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career so far? Beginning my first clinical trial and receiving my first grant as principal investigator have been the most exciting and rewarding milestones of my scientific career so far. Both represent pivotal steps toward establishing my independence as a researcher and advancing evidence-based nutrition care in inherited metabolic disorders. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would be dual-energy X-ray absorptiometer (DXA) scanner—it reveals what’s beneath the surface to give a clearer, more complete picture. My research takes a similar approach, integrating genotype, metabolism, and diet to inform personalized nutrition strategies that better support individuals with IMDs. articles.title: Postdoc Portrait: Ritu Ramamurthy Engineers Human Organoid Models
articles.text:
Skip to main content Interview Postdoc Portrait: Ritu Ramamurthy Engineers Human Organoid Models This postdoctoral fellow develops three-dimensional human organoid models to capture complex cellular interactions and improve translational drug development. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. It is read by leading researchers in industry and academia who value penetrating analyses and broad perspectives on life-science topics both within and beyond their areas of expertise. Written by prominent scientists and professional journalists, articles in are concise, accurate, accessible, and entertaining. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 18, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Ritu Ramamurthy designs 3D bioengineered environments to improve the physiological relevance of human cellular models. Image credit: © iStock.com , JuSun PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 2:00 Share Ritu Ramamurthy is a postdoctoral researcher studying human organoid models at Wake Forest University. In this Postdoc Portrait interview, she shares how she hopes to continue improving models that accurately represent human biology and translate into better treatments. Engineering Human Tissue Environments Q | What drew you to organoids? I was initially drawn to molecular biology because I’ve always been fascinated by how something as simple as combinations of four bases in DNA can give rise to incredibly complex living systems. The idea that tiny changes at the genetic level could completely alter human health made me want to understand biology at its most fundamental level. That curiosity led me towards gene therapy, where instead of only treating symptoms, we can potentially correct the root cause of a genetic disease. I found that concept incredibly powerful—that a single genetic intervention could have long-term therapeutic impact. As I became more involved in the field, I was looking for models where I could test genetic interventions with better accuracy and closer relevance to humans, while also reducing the use of animals . That is what drew me to organoids. Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve? I work on developing 3D human organoid models to study biological questions that are difficult to answer using traditional animal models alone. A lot of therapies look promising early on but fail later because current animal models fail to recapitulate true human responses. At the same time, 2D cultures are also limited , since cells in the body naturally exist and interact within dynamic 3D microenvironments. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe What I enjoy most about this work is that it combines engineering with biology. I get to help create environments where human cells can grow, communicate, and behave closer to how they function inside the body. I find that incredibly exciting because even small improvements in these models could help researchers make better decisions earlier in the drug development process and eventually improve how therapies reach patients. Overcoming Limitations in Translational Research Q | What’s one thing you learned from translational research that you didn’t expect? One thing I didn’t fully expect was just how much biological variability exists between humans and how strongly that can shape results. When I started working with primary human cells and organoid-based systems, I saw how dramatically the responses can vary depending on donor background and the microenvironment you provide. That experience changed the way I think about translational research. Animal models are incredibly valuable, but they often cannot capture the complexity and differences seen in human biology, since experimental animals are usually more uniform. It made me realize why so many promising therapies struggle during clinical translation. Instead of viewing variability as a problem to eliminate, I learned to see it as an essential part of understanding disease and treatment response. Q | What question are you most excited to answer next? Vasculature is a critical component of the body. However, its inclusion in organoid systems is still limited. I am particularly interested in improving ways to perfuse organoids to better support their structure and function. This could help enhance how closely these models recapitulate human tissue and potentially extend their lifespan, enabling the study of long-term biological interactions, which remains a major challenge in the field. I hope my research contributes to a future where therapies are developed faster, tested more reliably, and tailored more effectively to human biology. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Send in your application here . Webinar Sound the alarm on aggregation for proteins, peptides, and ADCs on Stunner In this webinar, Kevin Lance will discuss practical guidance for problematic samples and how to develop faster, sample-sparing, and downstream-friendly methods on Stunner. Share With a Colleague Share This webinar, brought to you by Unchained Labs, will be hosted live and available on-demand. Thursday, March 26, 2026 11:00 AM-12:00 PM ET Biotherapeutic pipelines don’t just juggle monoclonal antibodies anymore, they need to be ready for protein conjugates like ADCs and for teeny tiny peptides. Even beyond new modalities, automation is pushing throughput higher so that analytical tools for concentration and sizing need to read lots of samples as fast as possible, on the tiniest sample volumes, and with increasingly higher throughput. For ADCs, UV/Vis gets messy when payload absorbance overlaps the antibody signal, and aggregation risk increases as hydrophobic drug-linkers enter the mix. For peptides, answering the questions “How much do I have?” and “Has aggregation struck?” need to happen without turning characterization into a bottleneck. Stunner bundles quantification and sizing checks into a single read that can pinpoint sample concentration and raise the red flag when aggregation has struck. Full-spectrum UV/Vis quantifies while dynamic light scattering (DLS) checks size and aggregation on the same 2 µL sample in a 96-well plate-based format. Stunner’s Unmix algorithms power applications to deconvolute UV/Vis spectra into protein, drug-linkers, nucleic acids, or other components, making quantification and drug-antibody ratio (DAR) reads simpler than ever. Since UV/Vis is read in the same run as DLS, Stunner streamlines workflows for proteins, ADCs, and peptides with less handoffs and variability. In this webinar, brought to you by Unchained Labs, Kevin Lance will discuss how Stunner accelerates everything from routine sample checks and screening to final quality control across protein modalities. Kevin will present the technology behind rapid concentration and aggregation monitoring for proteins and peptides and the newest applications for fast DAR and concentration readouts for ADCs and other conjugates. Overall, this practical guidance will enable the identification of problematic samples and the knowledge to develop faster, sample-sparing, and downstream-friendly methods on Stunner. Topics to be covered Learn how full-spectrum UV/Vis and DLS can deliver concentration, size, and aggregation data from the same microvolume sample Understand common aggregation and quantification challenges across proteins, peptides, and ADCs See how spectral deconvolution simplifies ADC concentration and DAR measurements despite overlapping absorbance Gain practical guidance for developing Stunner workflows Kevin Lance, PhD Director, Product Management Unchained Labs Sponsored by Top Image Credit: iStock: Miyako Nakamura You might also be interested in... The Nutshell Deltaviruses Use a Trojan Horse Method to Spread New research shows that deltaviruses can sneak into cells by hitchhiking inside other viruses, highlighting a previously unrecognized transmission mode. Written by Laura Tran, PhD Laura Tran, PhD Laura is an Associate Editor for The Scientist . She has a background in microbiology. Her science communication work spans journalism and public engagement. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Mar 6, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Deltaviruses employ a “viral Trojan Horse” strategy to spread to new cells in different tissues. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Rost-9D PDF version Share V iruses are crafty and can employ different strategies to spread. One approach involves a virus relying on another “helper” virus to infect. A classic example is hepatitis delta virus (HDV), which hijacks hepatitis B virus surface glycoproteins to enter cells. In recent years, scientists have discovered many HDV-like deltaviruses in animals . While helper viral glycoproteins play a role in infectivity, the broader nature of deltavirus–helper virus interactions remained largely unresolved. This motivated researchers at the Institute of Molecular Genetics of Montpellier (IGMM) and the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS) to take a closer look at this relationship. Their findings, published in Cell , revealed a previously unrecognized mode of viral transmission. 1 Instead of hitchhiking on the surface of helper virus glycoproteins to enter cells, deltaviruses take a page out of an ancient Greek strategy—the Trojan Horse. Here, deltaviruses can package themselves inside another virus particle as a vehicle to spread and enter other cells. These findings reveal a novel viral transmission route and shed insight into how deltaviruses spread to different tissues. The researchers first infected murine cells with rodent deltavirus (RDeV) and then introduced a second infection with vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV). The team had previously shown that this deltavirus can replicate and be packaged within the helper virus’s glycoproteins to form infectious particles. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe They observed the characteristic bullet-shaped VSV virions, smaller RDeV particles, and a surprising third population in the cellular supernatant. The team suspected that this third group might be a subset of VSV virions that was morphologically modified and might contain RDeV particles. To investigate further, the researchers combined multiple imaging techniques, including electron microscopy and super-resolution microscopy, to confirm the presence of deltaviruses enclosed within the helper virus particles. Indeed, these deltaviruses were able to hitchhike inside VSV particles upon infection and gain entry to naive target cells. The researchers termed this mode of transmission as “viral Trojan Horse spread.” But this phenomenon does not only occur in VSV models. The team expanded their analysis to include other helper virus families, such as herpesvirus and arenaviruses; cell lines derived from different organs, including the liver and kidney; and cells from various species. They observed a similar pattern of Trojan Horse packaging across these conditions. Notably, this mechanism was required for productive deltavirus infection. Based on these findings, the researchers emphasized that more work is needed to identify these Trojan Horse particles in other viral settings. However, virologist Joe McKellar , a study coauthor at IGMM/CNRS, remarked in a press release, "It opens new questions about deltavirus tropism and evolution." References McKellar J, et al. Deltaviruses spread through a viral Trojan Horse . Cell . 2026. Khalfi P, et al. Comparative analysis of human, rodent and snake deltavirus replication . PLoS Pathog . 2024;20(3):e1012060. Add The Scientist as a preferred Google source to see more of our trusted coverage. Related Topics Cell & Molecular Biology , Immunology , infection , transmission , viral entry , Viral evolution Meet the Author Laura Tran, PhD Laura Tran is an Associate Editor, Content & Newsletters at The Scientist. She has a background in microbiology. Laura earned her PhD in integrated biomedical sciences from Rush University, studying how circadian rhythms and alcohol impact the gut. While completing her studies, she wrote for the Chicago Council on Science and Technology and participated in ComSciCon Chicago in 2022. In 2023, Laura became a science communication fellow with OMSI, continuing her passion for accessible science storytelling. At the same time, 2D cultures are also limited , since cells in the body naturally exist and interact within dynamic 3D microenvironments. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe What I enjoy most about this work is that it combines engineering with biology. I get to help create environments where human cells can grow, communicate, and behave closer to how they function inside the body. I find that incredibly exciting because even small improvements in these models could help researchers make better decisions earlier in the drug development process and eventually improve how therapies reach patients. Overcoming Limitations in Translational Research Q | What’s one thing you learned from translational research that you didn’t expect? One thing I didn’t fully expect was just how much biological variability exists between humans and how strongly that can shape results. When I started working with primary human cells and organoid-based systems, I saw how dramatically the responses can vary depending on donor background and the microenvironment you provide. That experience changed the way I think about translational research. Animal models are incredibly valuable, but they often cannot capture the complexity and differences seen in human biology, since experimental animals are usually more uniform. It made me realize why so many promising therapies struggle during clinical translation. Instead of viewing variability as a problem to eliminate, I learned to see it as an essential part of understanding disease and treatment response. Q | What question are you most excited to answer next? Vasculature is a critical component of the body. However, its inclusion in organoid systems is still limited. I am particularly interested in improving ways to perfuse organoids to better support their structure and function. This could help enhance how closely these models recapitulate human tissue and potentially extend their lifespan, enabling the study of long-term biological interactions, which remains a major challenge in the field. I hope my research contributes to a future where therapies are developed faster, tested more reliably, and tailored more effectively to human biology. Thursday, March 26, 2026 11:00 AM-12:00 PM ET Biotherapeutic pipelines don’t just juggle monoclonal antibodies anymore, they need to be ready for protein conjugates like ADCs and for teeny tiny peptides. Even beyond new modalities, automation is pushing throughput higher so that analytical tools for concentration and sizing need to read lots of samples as fast as possible, on the tiniest sample volumes, and with increasingly higher throughput. For ADCs, UV/Vis gets messy when payload absorbance overlaps the antibody signal, and aggregation risk increases as hydrophobic drug-linkers enter the mix. For peptides, answering the questions “How much do I have?” and “Has aggregation struck?” need to happen without turning characterization into a bottleneck. Stunner bundles quantification and sizing checks into a single read that can pinpoint sample concentration and raise the red flag when aggregation has struck. Full-spectrum UV/Vis quantifies while dynamic light scattering (DLS) checks size and aggregation on the same 2 µL sample in a 96-well plate-based format. Stunner’s Unmix algorithms power applications to deconvolute UV/Vis spectra into protein, drug-linkers, nucleic acids, or other components, making quantification and drug-antibody ratio (DAR) reads simpler than ever. Since UV/Vis is read in the same run as DLS, Stunner streamlines workflows for proteins, ADCs, and peptides with less handoffs and variability. In this webinar, brought to you by Unchained Labs, Kevin Lance will discuss how Stunner accelerates everything from routine sample checks and screening to final quality control across protein modalities. Kevin will present the technology behind rapid concentration and aggregation monitoring for proteins and peptides and the newest applications for fast DAR and concentration readouts for ADCs and other conjugates. Overall, this practical guidance will enable the identification of problematic samples and the knowledge to develop faster, sample-sparing, and downstream-friendly methods on Stunner. The Nutshell Deltaviruses Use a Trojan Horse Method to Spread New research shows that deltaviruses can sneak into cells by hitchhiking inside other viruses, highlighting a previously unrecognized transmission mode. Written by Laura Tran, PhD Laura Tran, PhD Laura is an Associate Editor for The Scientist . She has a background in microbiology. Her science communication work spans journalism and public engagement. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Mar 6, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Deltaviruses employ a “viral Trojan Horse” strategy to spread to new cells in different tissues. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Rost-9D PDF version Share V iruses are crafty and can employ different strategies to spread. One approach involves a virus relying on another “helper” virus to infect. A classic example is hepatitis delta virus (HDV), which hijacks hepatitis B virus surface glycoproteins to enter cells. In recent years, scientists have discovered many HDV-like deltaviruses in animals . While helper viral glycoproteins play a role in infectivity, the broader nature of deltavirus–helper virus interactions remained largely unresolved. This motivated researchers at the Institute of Molecular Genetics of Montpellier (IGMM) and the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS) to take a closer look at this relationship. Their findings, published in Cell , revealed a previously unrecognized mode of viral transmission. 1 Instead of hitchhiking on the surface of helper virus glycoproteins to enter cells, deltaviruses take a page out of an ancient Greek strategy—the Trojan Horse. Here, deltaviruses can package themselves inside another virus particle as a vehicle to spread and enter other cells. These findings reveal a novel viral transmission route and shed insight into how deltaviruses spread to different tissues. The researchers first infected murine cells with rodent deltavirus (RDeV) and then introduced a second infection with vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV). The team had previously shown that this deltavirus can replicate and be packaged within the helper virus’s glycoproteins to form infectious particles. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe They observed the characteristic bullet-shaped VSV virions, smaller RDeV particles, and a surprising third population in the cellular supernatant. The team suspected that this third group might be a subset of VSV virions that was morphologically modified and might contain RDeV particles. To investigate further, the researchers combined multiple imaging techniques, including electron microscopy and super-resolution microscopy, to confirm the presence of deltaviruses enclosed within the helper virus particles. Indeed, these deltaviruses were able to hitchhike inside VSV particles upon infection and gain entry to naive target cells. The researchers termed this mode of transmission as “viral Trojan Horse spread.” But this phenomenon does not only occur in VSV models. The team expanded their analysis to include other helper virus families, such as herpesvirus and arenaviruses; cell lines derived from different organs, including the liver and kidney; and cells from various species. They observed a similar pattern of Trojan Horse packaging across these conditions. Notably, this mechanism was required for productive deltavirus infection. Based on these findings, the researchers emphasized that more work is needed to identify these Trojan Horse particles in other viral settings. However, virologist Joe McKellar , a study coauthor at IGMM/CNRS, remarked in a press release, "It opens new questions about deltavirus tropism and evolution." References McKellar J, et al. Deltaviruses spread through a viral Trojan Horse . Cell . 2026. Khalfi P, et al. Comparative analysis of human, rodent and snake deltavirus replication . PLoS Pathog . 2024;20(3):e1012060. That curiosity led me towards gene therapy, where instead of only treating symptoms, we can potentially correct the root cause of a genetic disease. I found that concept incredibly powerful—that a single genetic intervention could have long-term therapeutic impact. As I became more involved in the field, I was looking for models where I could test genetic interventions with better accuracy and closer relevance to humans, while also reducing the use of animals . That is what drew me to organoids. Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve? A lot of therapies look promising early on but fail later because current animal models fail to recapitulate true human responses. Webinar
Sound the alarm on aggregation for proteins, peptides, and ADCs on Stunner
In this webinar, Kevin Lance will discuss practical guidance for problematic samples and how to develop faster, sample-sparing, and downstream-friendly methods on Stunner.
Share With a Colleague Share This webinar, brought to you by Unchained Labs, will be hosted live and available on-demand. Thursday, March 26, 2026 11:00 AM-12:00 PM ET Biotherapeutic pipelines don’t just juggle monoclonal antibodies anymore, they need to be ready for protein conjugates like ADCs and for teeny tiny peptides. Even beyond new modalities, automation is pushing throughput higher so that analytical tools for concentration and sizing need to read lots of samples as fast as possible, on the tiniest sample volumes, and with increasingly higher throughput. For ADCs, UV/Vis gets messy when payload absorbance overlaps the antibody signal, and aggregation risk increases as hydrophobic drug-linkers enter the mix. For peptides, answering the questions “How much do I have?” and “Has aggregation struck?” need to happen without turning characterization into a bottleneck. Stunner bundles quantification and sizing checks into a single read that can pinpoint sample concentration and raise the red flag when aggregation has struck. Full-spectrum UV/Vis quantifies while dynamic light scattering (DLS) checks size and aggregation on the same 2 µL sample in a 96-well plate-based format. Stunner’s Unmix algorithms power applications to deconvolute UV/Vis spectra into protein, drug-linkers, nucleic acids, or other components, making quantification and drug-antibody ratio (DAR) reads simpler than ever. Since UV/Vis is read in the same run as DLS, Stunner streamlines workflows for proteins, ADCs, and peptides with less handoffs and variability. Overall, this practical guidance will enable the identification of problematic samples and the knowledge to develop faster, sample-sparing, and downstream-friendly methods on Stunner. The Nutshell Deltaviruses Use a Trojan Horse Method to Spread New research shows that deltaviruses can sneak into cells by hitchhiking inside other viruses, highlighting a previously unrecognized transmission mode. Written by Laura Tran, PhD Laura Tran, PhD Laura is an Associate Editor for The Scientist . She has a background in microbiology. Her science communication work spans journalism and public engagement. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Mar 6, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Deltaviruses employ a “viral Trojan Horse” strategy to spread to new cells in different tissues. Image credit: ©iStock.com, Rost-9D PDF version Share V iruses are crafty and can employ different strategies to spread. One approach involves a virus relying on another “helper” virus to infect. A classic example is hepatitis delta virus (HDV), which hijacks hepatitis B virus surface glycoproteins to enter cells. In recent years, scientists have discovered many HDV-like deltaviruses in animals . While helper viral glycoproteins play a role in infectivity, the broader nature of deltavirus–helper virus interactions remained largely unresolved. This motivated researchers at the Institute of Molecular Genetics of Montpellier (IGMM) and the French National Center for Scientific Research (CNRS) to take a closer look at this relationship. Their findings, published in Cell , revealed a previously unrecognized mode of viral transmission. 1 Instead of hitchhiking on the surface of helper virus glycoproteins to enter cells, deltaviruses take a page out of an ancient Greek strategy—the Trojan Horse. Here, deltaviruses can package themselves inside another virus particle as a vehicle to spread and enter other cells. These findings reveal a novel viral transmission route and shed insight into how deltaviruses spread to different tissues. The researchers first infected murine cells with rodent deltavirus (RDeV) and then introduced a second infection with vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV). The team had previously shown that this deltavirus can replicate and be packaged within the helper virus’s glycoproteins to form infectious particles. 2 Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe They observed the characteristic bullet-shaped VSV virions, smaller RDeV particles, and a surprising third population in the cellular supernatant. The team suspected that this third group might be a subset of VSV virions that was morphologically modified and might contain RDeV particles. To investigate further, the researchers combined multiple imaging techniques, including electron microscopy and super-resolution microscopy, to confirm the presence of deltaviruses enclosed within the helper virus particles. Indeed, these deltaviruses were able to hitchhike inside VSV particles upon infection and gain entry to naive target cells. The researchers termed this mode of transmission as “viral Trojan Horse spread.” But this phenomenon does not only occur in VSV models. The team expanded their analysis to include other helper virus families, such as herpesvirus and arenaviruses; cell lines derived from different organs, including the liver and kidney; and cells from various species. They observed a similar pattern of Trojan Horse packaging across these conditions. Notably, this mechanism was required for productive deltavirus infection. Based on these findings, the researchers emphasized that more work is needed to identify these Trojan Horse particles in other viral settings. However, virologist Joe McKellar , a study coauthor at IGMM/CNRS, remarked in a press release, "It opens new questions about deltavirus tropism and evolution." References McKellar J, et al. Deltaviruses spread through a viral Trojan Horse . Cell . 2026. Khalfi P, et al. Comparative analysis of human, rodent and snake deltavirus replication . PLoS Pathog . 2024;20(3):e1012060. But this phenomenon does not only occur in VSV models. articles.title: Then and Now: Cigall Kadoch Tracks Down Chromatin Remodelers in Cancer
articles.text:
Skip to main content Article Then and Now: Cigall Kadoch Tracks Down Chromatin Remodelers in Cancer As a biochemist and molecular biologist, Kadoch investigates how cancer hijacks chromatin remodeling complexes in synovial sarcoma and other cancers to identify new treatments. Written by Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Go to The Scientist's LinkedIn Page Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga is a Brazilian freelance science writer, who covers the quirky and fascinating biology of cells and animals. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 16, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Biochemist and molecular biologist Cigall Kadoch has dedicated her scientific career to characterizing the BAF chromatin remodeling complexes, uncovering how they are co-opted in cancer to drive disease. Image credit: © KEN RICHARDSON PHOTOGRAPHY, Ken Richardson, Modified by Erin Lemieux PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share T aking unconventional paths with no fear of exploring the unknown has been a hallmark of Cigall Kadoch ’s work. As a graduate student, the now biochemist and molecular biologist joined a developmental biology lab to study cancer. While the decision may have seemed counterintuitive to some, it was not to Kadoch. “Cancer, in essence, is development gone wrong,” she explained. “Cancer is a hijacking, or an aberrant modulation, of the very pathways that control development.” This mindset guided Kadoch into the world of chromatin remodeling complexes , large protein machines that regulate chromatin accessibility and gene expression. As a postdoctoral researcher, she discovered that a single translocation of 78 amino acids to one subunit of the BRG1/BRM-associated factor (BAF) chromatin remodeler causes the loss of tumor suppression in a rare and aggressive form of pediatric cancer known as synovial sarcoma, turning cells cancerous. 1,2 Almost 10 years after The Scientist first spoke to Kadoch about her research, we reached out again to find out what uncharted scientific paths she has now forged to reveal the secrets of BAF complexes. Kadoch and her team have dissected the mechanisms that drive these complexes to act aberrantly in cancer, uncovering functional changes that are informing new therapeutic approaches. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe BAF Complexes Under the Scope BAF complexes are master regulators that modulate chromatin accessibility and binding of the transcriptional machinery that drives gene expression. 3 A cell can express a variety of BAF complexes, which are assembled from a combination of 11 to 15 subunits pieced together from the products of 29 genes. These chromatin remodelers are mutated in more than 20 percent of human cancers, and Kadoch explained that this might be due to their crucial roles in maintaining cell identity and proper developmental processes. 4 BAF complexes are large protein machines that render the chromatin accessible to transcriptional machinery and help regulate gene expression. Kadoch lab In synovial sarcoma, SS18, a subunit of the BAF complex, fuses with another protein called SSX, forming a fusion oncoprotein. This SS18-SSX fusion binds to the BAF complex and expels both the wild type SS18 and a tumor suppressor subunit, causing the chromatin remodeler to act aberrantly. Even though researchers knew this fusion oncoprotein played a key role in synovial sarcoma, the exact genomic locations where the SS18-SSX containing BAF complexes went to cause the cancer-associated changes were unknown. Using both synovial sarcoma cell lines and samples from patients, Kadoch’s team found that the SS18-SSX containing chromatin remodelers targeted genes that were normally repressed by polycomb repressive complexes (PRCs), leading to the eviction of PRCs and expression of genes that should not be active. 5 “That really established this cancer-specific hijacking of the BAF complex,” Kadoch said. While the new findings showed where the altered BAF complexes bound to the chromatin, why these complexes moved to these sites in the first place still puzzled Kadoch. The primary difference between a regular and aberrant BAF complex is the little 78-amino acid long fusion protein tail, which drags the entire chromatin remodeler to the genomic sites it should not bind to, she explained. Her team discovered that the oncogenic fusion protein interacts with chromatin and directs the BAF complex by binding it to the acid region of nucleosomes bearing a specific mark placed by PRCs, thus unveiling the motif that attracts the fusion oncoprotein in synovial sarcoma. 6 BAF Complexes Hijacking Beyond Synovial Sarcoma That PRCs placed marks on nucleosomes uncovered another piece of the puzzle of how BAF remodelers navigate to specific sites on the two-meter-long human DNA, but Kadoch and her team still wondered whether other mechanisms were at play. The researchers knew that transcription factors can be overexpressed in a cancer-dependent manner, and BAF complexes often co-localized with these highly expressed factors. This evidence led the researchers to wonder whether there was a direct interaction between the chromatin remodelers and abundant transcription factors. The team found that highly expressed transcription factors bind to a specific domain on one of the core subunits of BAF complexes and that this interaction influences where the complexes land along the chromatin. 7 Continue reading below... Article Eliminate Bottlenecks in Checkpoint Inhibitor Analysis A high-throughput cytometry workflow consolidates marker expression, proliferation, and cytokine release into a single assay plate. Read More Describing the roles of the different BAF subunits and tracking the mechanisms involved in the hijacking of these complexes allowed Kadoch’s team to expand their studies on chromatin remodelers to other cancers, including lung cancer and endometrial carcinomas. 8,9 It also made Kadoch realize the vast therapeutic potential of modulating the activity of these complexes—an area she has been exploring at Foghorn Therapeutics, a biotechnology company she helped found, which aims to identify components in the chromatin regulatory system, including complex subunits and associated transcription factors that can be targeted to treat cancer and other BAF complex-associated diseases. Kadoch’s entire scientific journey has felt unexpected, but she has adopted a “no-fear approach” to pursue and discover something new. “That's what one has to be ready for in research. We have to be open and receptive to the unknown in what we find and where that leads us, and also open and receptive to the career paths that may come from certain findings and discoveries along the way.” References Kadoch C, Crabtree GR. Reversible disruption of mSWI/SNF (BAF) complexes by the SS18-SSX oncogenic fusion in synovial sarcoma . Cell . 2013;153(1):71-85. Kadoch C, et al. Proteomic and bioinformatic analysis of mammalian SWI/SNF complexes identifies extensive roles in human malignancy . Nat Genet . 2013;45(6):592-601. Cramer P. Organization and regulation of gene transcription . Nature . 2019;573(7772):45-54. Shain AH, Pollack JR. The spectrum of SWI/SNF mutations, ubiquitous in human cancers . PLoS One . 2013;8(1):e55119. McBride MJ, et al. The SS18-SSX Fusion Oncoprotein Hijacks BAF Complex Targeting and Function to Drive Synovial Sarcoma . Cancer Cell . 2018;33(6):1128-1141.e7. McBride MJ, et al. The nucleosome acidic patch and H2A ubiquitination underlie mSWI/SNF recruitment in synovial sarcoma . Nat Struct Mol Biol . 2020;27(9):836-845. Jain SU, et al. A SWI/SNF-specific Ig-like domain, SWIFT, is a transcription factor binding platform . Science . 2026;392(6793):eaeb3627. Duplaquet L, et al. Mammalian SWI/SNF complex activity regulates POU2F3 and constitutes a targetable dependency in small cell lung cancer . Cancer Cell. 2024;42(8):1352-1369.e13. St Laurent JD, et al. Shifted assembly and function of mSWI/SNF family subcomplexes underlie targetable dependencies in dedifferentiated endometrial carcinomas . Nat Genet . 2025;57(11):2743-2755.
