Share 3dGRO ® Human iPSC-derived Liver Progenitor Organoids are derived from integration-free human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC, Cat No. SCC271 ) reprogrammed using the Simplicon RNA reprogramming technology (Cat. No. SCR550 ). Human iPSC cells were differentiated using a multi-step process from definitive endoderm to hepatic endoderm and eventually into liver progenitor organoids. These liver progenitor organoids are polarized self-organizing, 3D structures that can be expanded in long-term culture and cryo-preservable. These liver progenitor organoids show persistent growth and expansion post cryo-preservation. 3dGRO ® human iPSC-derived liver progenitor organoids (Cat. No. SCC572 ) can be propagated long-term by passaging every 8-10 days using 3dGRO ® Human Liver Progenitor Organoid Expansion Media (Cat. no. SCM313 ). Interview Postdoc Portrait: Brandon Reagan This postdoctoral researcher investigates how plant cells balance growth with responses to endoplasmic reticulum stress. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. It is read by leading researchers in industry and academia who value penetrating analyses and broad perspectives on life-science topics both within and beyond their areas of expertise. Written by prominent scientists and professional journalists, articles in are concise, accurate, accessible, and entertaining. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Mar 9, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Brandon Reagan uses advanced imaging to study how plant cells manage the stress response within the endoplasmic reticulum. Image credit: © iStock.com , R_Koopmans PDF version Share Brandon Reagan is a postdoctoral researcher working in the lab of Federica Brandizzi at Michigan State University. He is a plant cell biologist studying how cells respond to stress and how plants establish a balance between growth and stress responses. In this Postdoc Portrait, he shares his research journey and how his current project incorporates his love of microscopy. Falling in Love with the Living Cell Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? While I was interested in biology broadly from a young age, it was not until I was an undergraduate researcher working in a lab at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville that discovered my passion for cell biology. I participated in a National Science Foundation-funded research experience for undergraduates and I studied cell division in Caenorhabditis elegans . During this program, I had the opportunity to use my first confocal microscope and got to watch a cell divide before my very eyes. As I watched the mitotic spindle pull the chromosomes apart, I fell in love with cell biology and microscopy in general. Balancing Growth and Stress Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. My favorite project that I am currently working on seeks to understand how specialized proteins that maintain the structure of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) regulate responses to ER stress and autophagy. I love this project because it combines my love for live cell imaging with various biochemical and molecular approaches to address an important, yet challenging biological question. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific journey so far? The most exciting part of my career thus far has been having the opportunity to travel, meet, and work with scientists from around the world. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would definitely be a microscope. I have spent many hours looking through the eyepieces of different microscopes and I can honestly say that it is my favorite part of my job, and I hope that never stops. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Send in your application here . Interpreting CRISPR Screens Through Protein-Level Biology Amanda McQuade and Nathaniel Robichaud discuss how integrated proteomic and transcriptomic profiling can reveal cellular responses to genetic perturbations that are not captured through RNA analysis alone. Translating Cancer Biomarker Research Integrating broad scope high-throughput omics technologies with targeted analytical validation assays enables translational cancer biomarker research workflows. Advancing Microbiology Research with Real-Time Measurements Using microplate readers and reporter assays, scientists can precisely monitor pathogen growth, gene expression, and response to treatment over time. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. Mar 9, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Brandon Reagan uses advanced imaging to study how plant cells manage the stress response within the endoplasmic reticulum. Image credit: © iStock.com , R_Koopmans PDF version Share Brandon Reagan is a postdoctoral researcher working in the lab of Federica Brandizzi at Michigan State University. He is a plant cell biologist studying how cells respond to stress and how plants establish a balance between growth and stress responses. In this Postdoc Portrait, he shares his research journey and how his current project incorporates his love of microscopy. Falling in Love with the Living Cell Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? While I was interested in biology broadly from a young age, it was not until I was an undergraduate researcher working in a lab at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville that discovered my passion for cell biology. I participated in a National Science Foundation-funded research experience for undergraduates and I studied cell division in Caenorhabditis elegans . During this program, I had the opportunity to use my first confocal microscope and got to watch a cell divide before my very eyes. As I watched the mitotic spindle pull the chromosomes apart, I fell in love with cell biology and microscopy in general. Balancing Growth and Stress Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. My favorite project that I am currently working on seeks to understand how specialized proteins that maintain the structure of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) regulate responses to ER stress and autophagy. I love this project because it combines my love for live cell imaging with various biochemical and molecular approaches to address an important, yet challenging biological question. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific journey so far? The most exciting part of my career thus far has been having the opportunity to travel, meet, and work with scientists from around the world. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would definitely be a microscope. I have spent many hours looking through the eyepieces of different microscopes and I can honestly say that it is my favorite part of my job, and I hope that never stops. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Send in your application here . Article Then and Now: Cigall Kadoch Tracks Down Chromatin Remodelers in Cancer As a biochemist and molecular biologist, Kadoch investigates how cancer hijacks chromatin remodeling complexes in synovial sarcoma and other cancers to identify new treatments. Written by Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga, PhD Go to The Scientist's LinkedIn Page Mariella Bodemeier Loayza Careaga is a Brazilian freelance science writer, who covers the quirky and fascinating biology of cells and animals. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 16, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Biochemist and molecular biologist Cigall Kadoch has dedicated her scientific career to characterizing the BAF chromatin remodeling complexes, uncovering how they are co-opted in cancer to drive disease. Image credit: © KEN RICHARDSON PHOTOGRAPHY, Ken Richardson, Modified by Erin Lemieux PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share T aking unconventional paths with no fear of exploring the unknown has been a hallmark of Cigall Kadoch ’s work. As a graduate student, the now biochemist and molecular biologist joined a developmental biology lab to study cancer. While the decision may have seemed counterintuitive to some, it was not to Kadoch. “Cancer, in essence, is development gone wrong,” she explained. “Cancer is a hijacking, or an aberrant modulation, of the very pathways that control development.” This mindset guided Kadoch into the world of chromatin remodeling complexes , large protein machines that regulate chromatin accessibility and gene expression. As a postdoctoral researcher, she discovered that a single translocation of 78 amino acids to one subunit of the BRG1/BRM-associated factor (BAF) chromatin remodeler causes the loss of tumor suppression in a rare and aggressive form of pediatric cancer known as synovial sarcoma, turning cells cancerous. 1,2 Almost 10 years after The Scientist first spoke to Kadoch about her research, we reached out again to find out what uncharted scientific paths she has now forged to reveal the secrets of BAF complexes. Kadoch and her team have dissected the mechanisms that drive these complexes to act aberrantly in cancer, uncovering functional changes that are informing new therapeutic approaches. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe BAF Complexes Under the Scope BAF complexes are master regulators that modulate chromatin accessibility and binding of the transcriptional machinery that drives gene expression. 3 A cell can express a variety of BAF complexes, which are assembled from a combination of 11 to 15 subunits pieced together from the products of 29 genes. These chromatin remodelers are mutated in more than 20 percent of human cancers, and Kadoch explained that this might be due to their crucial roles in maintaining cell identity and proper developmental processes. 4 BAF complexes are large protein machines that render the chromatin accessible to transcriptional machinery and help regulate gene expression. Kadoch lab In synovial sarcoma, SS18, a subunit of the BAF complex, fuses with another protein called SSX, forming a fusion oncoprotein. This SS18-SSX fusion binds to the BAF complex and expels both the wild type SS18 and a tumor suppressor subunit, causing the chromatin remodeler to act aberrantly. Even though researchers knew this fusion oncoprotein played a key role in synovial sarcoma, the exact genomic locations where the SS18-SSX containing BAF complexes went to cause the cancer-associated changes were unknown. Using both synovial sarcoma cell lines and samples from patients, Kadoch’s team found that the SS18-SSX containing chromatin remodelers targeted genes that were normally repressed by polycomb repressive complexes (PRCs), leading to the eviction of PRCs and expression of genes that should not be active. 5 “That really established this cancer-specific hijacking of the BAF complex,” Kadoch said. While the new findings showed where the altered BAF complexes bound to the chromatin, why these complexes moved to these sites in the first place still puzzled Kadoch. The primary difference between a regular and aberrant BAF complex is the little 78-amino acid long fusion protein tail, which drags the entire chromatin remodeler to the genomic sites it should not bind to, she explained. Her team discovered that the oncogenic fusion protein interacts with chromatin and directs the BAF complex by binding it to the acid region of nucleosomes bearing a specific mark placed by PRCs, thus unveiling the motif that attracts the fusion oncoprotein in synovial sarcoma. 6 BAF Complexes Hijacking Beyond Synovial Sarcoma That PRCs placed marks on nucleosomes uncovered another piece of the puzzle of how BAF remodelers navigate to specific sites on the two-meter-long human DNA, but Kadoch and her team still wondered whether other mechanisms were at play. The researchers knew that transcription factors can be overexpressed in a cancer-dependent manner, and BAF complexes often co-localized with these highly expressed factors. This evidence led the researchers to wonder whether there was a direct interaction between the chromatin remodelers and abundant transcription factors. The team found that highly expressed transcription factors bind to a specific domain on one of the core subunits of BAF complexes and that this interaction influences where the complexes land along the chromatin. 7 Continue reading below... Article Eliminate Bottlenecks in Checkpoint Inhibitor Analysis A high-throughput cytometry workflow consolidates marker expression, proliferation, and cytokine release into a single assay plate. Read More Describing the roles of the different BAF subunits and tracking the mechanisms involved in the hijacking of these complexes allowed Kadoch’s team to expand their studies on chromatin remodelers to other cancers, including lung cancer and endometrial carcinomas. 8,9 It also made Kadoch realize the vast therapeutic potential of modulating the activity of these complexes—an area she has been exploring at Foghorn Therapeutics, a biotechnology company she helped found, which aims to identify components in the chromatin regulatory system, including complex subunits and associated transcription factors that can be targeted to treat cancer and other BAF complex-associated diseases. Kadoch’s entire scientific journey has felt unexpected, but she has adopted a “no-fear approach” to pursue and discover something new. “That's what one has to be ready for in research. We have to be open and receptive to the unknown in what we find and where that leads us, and also open and receptive to the career paths that may come from certain findings and discoveries along the way.” References Kadoch C, Crabtree GR. This SS18-SSX fusion binds to the BAF complex and expels both the wild type SS18 and a tumor suppressor subunit, causing the chromatin remodeler to act aberrantly. Even though researchers knew this fusion oncoprotein played a key role in synovial sarcoma, the exact genomic locations where the SS18-SSX containing BAF complexes went to cause the cancer-associated changes were unknown. Using both synovial sarcoma cell lines and samples from patients, Kadoch’s team found that the SS18-SSX containing chromatin remodelers targeted genes that were normally repressed by polycomb repressive complexes (PRCs), leading to the eviction of PRCs and expression of genes that should not be active. 5 “That really established this cancer-specific hijacking of the BAF complex,” Kadoch said.
5
While the new findings showed where the altered BAF complexes bound to the chromatin, why these complexes moved to these sites in the first place still puzzled Kadoch. The primary difference between a regular and aberrant BAF complex is the little 78-amino acid long fusion protein tail, which drags the entire chromatin remodeler to the genomic sites it should not bind to, she explained. Her team discovered that the oncogenic fusion protein interacts with chromatin and directs the BAF complex by binding it to the acid region of nucleosomes bearing a specific mark placed by PRCs, thus unveiling the motif that attracts the fusion oncoprotein in synovial sarcoma. 6
6
BAF Complexes Hijacking Beyond Synovial Sarcoma
That PRCs placed marks on nucleosomes uncovered another piece of the puzzle of how BAF remodelers navigate to specific sites on the two-meter-long human DNA, but Kadoch and her team still wondered whether other mechanisms were at play. The researchers knew that transcription factors can be overexpressed in a cancer-dependent manner, and BAF complexes often co-localized with these highly expressed factors. This evidence led the researchers to wonder whether there was a direct interaction between the chromatin remodelers and abundant transcription factors. The team found that highly expressed transcription factors bind to a specific domain on one of the core subunits of BAF complexes and that this interaction influences where the complexes land along the chromatin. 7
7

Describing the roles of the different BAF subunits and tracking the mechanisms involved in the hijacking of these complexes allowed Kadoch’s team to expand their studies on chromatin remodelers to other cancers, including lung cancer and endometrial carcinomas. 8,9 It also made Kadoch realize the vast therapeutic potential of modulating the activity of these complexes—an area she has been exploring at Foghorn Therapeutics, a biotechnology company she helped found, which aims to identify components in the chromatin regulatory system, including complex subunits and associated transcription factors that can be targeted to treat cancer and other BAF complex-associated diseases.
8,9
therapeutic potential
Kadoch’s entire scientific journey has felt unexpected, but she has adopted a “no-fear approach” to pursue and discover something new. “That's what one has to be ready for in research. We have to be open and receptive to the unknown in what we find and where that leads us, and also open and receptive to the career paths that may come from certain findings and discoveries along the way.”

Mar 9, 2026 | 2 min read Save for Later Brandon Reagan uses advanced imaging to study how plant cells manage the stress response within the endoplasmic reticulum. Image credit: © iStock.com , R_Koopmans PDF version Share Brandon Reagan is a postdoctoral researcher working in the lab of Federica Brandizzi at Michigan State University. He is a plant cell biologist studying how cells respond to stress and how plants establish a balance between growth and stress responses. In this Postdoc Portrait, he shares his research journey and how his current project incorporates his love of microscopy. Falling in Love with the Living Cell Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research? While I was interested in biology broadly from a young age, it was not until I was an undergraduate researcher working in a lab at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville that discovered my passion for cell biology. I participated in a National Science Foundation-funded research experience for undergraduates and I studied cell division in Caenorhabditis elegans . During this program, I had the opportunity to use my first confocal microscope and got to watch a cell divide before my very eyes. As I watched the mitotic spindle pull the chromosomes apart, I fell in love with cell biology and microscopy in general. Balancing Growth and Stress Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. My favorite project that I am currently working on seeks to understand how specialized proteins that maintain the structure of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) regulate responses to ER stress and autophagy. I love this project because it combines my love for live cell imaging with various biochemical and molecular approaches to address an important, yet challenging biological question. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific journey so far? The most exciting part of my career thus far has been having the opportunity to travel, meet, and work with scientists from around the world. Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why? I would definitely be a microscope. I have spent many hours looking through the eyepieces of different microscopes and I can honestly say that it is my favorite part of my job, and I hope that never stops. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Send in your application here . Q | How did you first get interested in your field of research?
Q |
While I was interested in biology broadly from a young age, it was not until I was an undergraduate researcher working in a lab at the University of Tennessee-Knoxville that discovered my passion for cell biology. I participated in a National Science Foundation-funded research experience for undergraduates and I studied cell division in Caenorhabditis elegans . During this program, I had the opportunity to use my first confocal microscope and got to watch a cell divide before my very eyes. As I watched the mitotic spindle pull the chromosomes apart, I fell in love with cell biology and microscopy in general.
Caenorhabditis elegans
Balancing Growth and Stress
Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on.
My favorite project that I am currently working on seeks to understand how specialized proteins that maintain the structure of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) regulate responses to ER stress and autophagy. I love this project because it combines my love for live cell imaging with various biochemical and molecular approaches to address an important, yet challenging biological question.
endoplasmic reticulum
Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific journey so far?
The most exciting part of my career thus far has been having the opportunity to travel, meet, and work with scientists from around the world.
Q | If you could be a laboratory instrument, which one would you be and why?
I would definitely be a microscope. I have spent many hours looking through the eyepieces of different microscopes and I can honestly say that it is my favorite part of my job, and I hope that never stops.
Responses have been edited for length and clarity.

articles.title: Postdoc Portrait: Kaushik Chanda Studies Circular RNA in Neurodegeneration
articles.text:
Skip to main content Interview Postdoc Portrait: Kaushik Chanda Studies Circular RNA in Neurodegeneration This postdoctoral fellow investigates how long non-coding RNAs regulate mitochondrial behavior in neurodegenerative disease models. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. It is read by leading researchers in industry and academia who value penetrating analyses and broad perspectives on life-science topics both within and beyond their areas of expertise. Written by prominent scientists and professional journalists, articles in are concise, accurate, accessible, and entertaining. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Jun 16, 2026 | 4 min read Save for Later Kaushik Chanda explores the impact of non-coding RNA transcripts on dendritic spine density using fluorescence microscopy. Image credit: Scott A., © iStock.com , Alena Butusava PDF version Register for free to listen to this article Listen with   Speechify 0:00 4:00 Share Kaushik Chanda is a postdoctoral researcher at The Herbert Wertheim UF Scripps Institute for Biomedical Innovation & Technology (Scripps Research). He studies how long non-coding RNAs drive structural plasticity and mitochondrial homeostasis, with implications for preserving cognitive function in conditions like Alzheimer’s disease and Huntington’s disease. In this Postdoc Portrait interview, he shares how he hopes to bridge several fields of biology and reframe the regulatory role of these non-coding nucleic acids. Uncovering Non-coding RNA Functions in Synapses Q | What drew you to circular RNAs? I was drawn to the field of non-coding RNAs and neuronal biology by a fascination with how complex brain functions can be regulated by molecules once considered non-functional. During my research experience, I became particularly interested in the intersection of RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and synaptic plasticity because these processes are central to learning, memory, and neurodegenerative disease. Despite major advances in neuroscience, many mechanisms underlying neuronal adaptability and vulnerability remain unresolved, especially the role of circular RNAs (circRNAs) in regulating cellular organelles and synaptic function. My work on circRNAs emerged from this curiosity. Mitochondria are critical for neuronal energy demands and synaptic remodeling, yet little is known about how circRNAs influence mitochondrial behavior in neurons . The possibility that a stable, brain-enriched circRNA could directly modulate mitochondrial dynamics and neuronal plasticity opened an exciting and largely unexplored area of research. I am motivated by the opportunity to uncover new biological mechanisms that could ultimately contribute to therapies or biomarkers for neurological disorders. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve? CircRNAs are highly stable and abundant in neurons, yet the biological functions of most brain-enriched circRNAs remain unknown. Neurons rely heavily on precise mitochondrial transport, morphology, and bioenergetic balance to support synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. However, the molecular mechanisms linking non-coding RNAs to mitochondrial behavior in neurons are poorly understood. My research focuses on understanding how nuclear encoded mitochondrial circRNAs regulate neuronal and synaptic function through control of mitochondrial dynamics and local energy homeostasis. I aim to solve the fundamental problem of how circRNA contributes to neuronal health and plasticity, and whether their deregulation leads to synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration. Using neuronal models, biochemical assays, live-cell imaging, and functional studies, I investigate how circRNA influences mitochondrial stability, trafficking, and activity-dependent remodeling at synapses. This work could uncover a previously unrecognized layer of RNA-mediated mitochondrial regulation in the nervous system and identify circRNA as a potential therapeutic target or biomarker for neurological disorders associated with mitochondrial dysfunction. Target-Based Drug Discovery for Neurodegeneration Q | What have you learned from studying circRNA that you didn’t expect? One of the most unexpected things I learned from studying circRNA was how strongly mitochondrial behavior is linked to rapid synaptic remodeling in neurons. I initially approached circRNAs as gene regulatory molecules that would primarily influence transcriptional or post-transcriptional pathways. However, during my experiments, it became clear that altering circRNA levels could quickly affect mitochondrial stability, distribution, and dynamics within dendrites, ultimately influencing spine plasticity. This revealed a much more immediate and functional connection between non-coding RNAs and neuronal energetics than I had anticipated. Another surprising insight was the versatility of circRNAs themselves. Traditionally viewed as relatively passive regulators or microRNA sponges, circRNAs may play far more active roles in coordinating organelle function and cellular responses to neuronal activity. This shifted the way I think about RNA biology—not as isolated layers of regulation, but as integrated components of cellular architecture and signaling. The project taught me the importance of exploring unconventional hypotheses, especially in fields where many fundamental mechanisms remain unknown. Q | If your research succeeds, what could it change for science or society? This work could fundamentally expand our understanding of how non-coding RNAs regulate neuronal function and mitochondrial biology. While circRNAs are abundant in the brain, their physiological roles remain largely unexplored. Demonstrating that a circRNA actively controls mitochondrial dynamics and synaptic plasticity would uncover a new layer of gene regulation that links RNA biology directly to neuronal energy homeostasis and brain function. This could have important implications for diseases where mitochondrial dysfunction and synaptic degeneration are central features, including Alzheimer’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis , Parkinson’s disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders. By identifying circRNA-mediated pathways, the research may reveal novel therapeutic targets that can stabilize neuronal health or improve synaptic resilience. In the long term, circRNAs could also serve as minimally invasive biomarkers because of their remarkable stability in biological fluids. More broadly, this work could shift how scientists think about “non-coding” regions of the genome, highlighting that these RNAs are not passive byproducts, but active regulators of cellular organization, communication, and adaptation in the nervous system. Continue reading below... Product Axygen® MaxRecovery+™ Low Retention Pipette Tips: Because Every Drop Counts Precision matters. Axygen MaxRecovery+ pipette tips ensure maximum sample recovery and minimal waste, supporting your most demanding applications in drug discovery, molecular biology, and genomics. Read More Q | What question are you most excited to answer next? I want to know how circRNA mechanistically communicates with mitochondria to regulate synaptic plasticity. While we are beginning to observe clear effects on mitochondrial stability and neuronal remodeling, the precise molecular pathway remains unknown. I am particularly interested in determining whether this circRNA acts through specific RNA-binding proteins, microRNA networks, or direct interactions with mitochondrial transport and dynamics machinery such as kinesins and fission-fusion regulators. I am also excited to explore whether the circRNA functions as an activity-dependent regulator during learning-like neuronal stimulation and whether its dysregulation contributes to neurodegenerative disease progression. Another important direction is investigating whether mitochondrial-associated circRNAs can be detected in extracellular vesicles or blood, potentially serving as biomarkers for neurological disorders. Answering these questions could bridge several fields—RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and neuroscience—and help define a broader principle of how non-coding RNAs coordinate cellular energetics with synaptic adaptation. The possibility of uncovering an entirely new regulatory mechanism is what motivates me most. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Product PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler: Leading PCR performance and productivity for modern labs Looking for the best PCR thermal cycler? The PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler delivers precise temperature gradient control, flexible workflows, and productivity features to help labs achieve reliable results and work more efficiently. Share PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler: Leading PCR performance and productivity for modern labs The PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler is an advanced PCR thermal cycler designed for researchers who require precise, reproducible DNA amplification and flexible workflow capabilities. For laboratories evaluating the best PCR thermal cycler, PowerFlex combines high thermal accuracy, fast ramp rates, and multi-format compatibility to support a wide range of molecular biology applications. Built on decades of PCR innovation, the Applied Biosystems™ PowerFlex Thermal Cycler delivers consistent thermal cycling performance, supports multiple experiment types, and improves lab efficiency through intuitive controls and remote connectivity. Performance: High-precision temperature gradient control for reliable results Achieving consistent, high-quality PCR results starts with precise temperature control. The PowerFlex Thermal Cycler features advanced VeriFlex™ block technology, delivering thermal accuracy of ±0.15°C and uniformity of 0.4°C across wells. Fast ramp rates of up to 7°C per second reduce cycle times while maintaining amplification quality and reaction integrity, enabling higher throughput without compromising data quality. The optimized block design ensures uniform heating and cooling, reducing variability and improving reproducibility across experiments. This level of precision supports a wide range of applications—including standard PCR and assay optimization—and provides researchers with more reliable amplification, improved data consistency, and greater confidence in experimental outcomes. Compatibility: flexible configurations for diverse applications PowerFlex supports a wide range of PCR consumables—including non-skirted, semi-skirted, and fully skirted plates, as well as tubes and other plastics formats—helping modern laboratories adapt to evolving workflows. The system offers multiple block configurations, including 96-well and 3 × 32-well formats, allowing researchers to run different experiments simultaneously. Independent temperature zones enable optimization of multiple assays in a single run, reducing the need for additional instruments. This flexibility allows labs to standardize workflows while maintaining the ability to adapt to new protocols and experimental requirements. Productivity: Streamlined workflows Efficiency is essential in high-throughput and multi-user lab environments. PowerFlex features a redesigned user interface on a 10.1-inch touchscreen, delivering clear visibility and intuitive navigation for faster setup and experiment monitoring. Independent block temperature control enables multiple experiments or users to run concurrently, improving instrument utilization, while a compact footprint helps conserve valuable bench space. Integrated Smart Help functionality allows users to submit support requests directly from the instrument, eliminating the need to step away from the lab bench. Instrument data can be shared with the service team, at the user’s discretion, to enable more accurate troubleshooting—reducing downtime and saving valuable research time. Remote access through Thermo Fisher Connect lets users to upload methods, monitor runs, and manage experiments from a computer or mobile device. Delivering confidence in every PCR run For researchers seeking the best PCR thermal cycler, the PowerFlex™ Thermal Cycler delivers a strong balance of precision, flexibility, and operational efficiency. Designed as a high-performance PCR system, it provides consistent, reproducible results while supporting diverse workflows across a range of applications. PowerFlex enables researchers to optimize experiments, improve throughput, and generate high-quality DNA amplification data with confidence—making it a valuable addition to any modern molecular biology laboratory. Weight 13 kg (28.7 lb) 15 kg (33 lb) PCR volume range 1–100 µL VeriFlex Blocks range 6 temperature zones (up to 10°C zone-to-zone), 30°C range across the block 2 temperature zones (up to 10°C zone-to-zone) Smart Help Yes Sponsored by Top Image Credit: ©iStock, anyaivanova You might also be interested in... Interview Postdoc Portrait: Anurag Roy As a material chemist, this postdoc explores how design at the molecular level can improve renewable energy technologies. Written by The Scientist The Scientist The Scientist is the magazine for life science professionals—a publication dedicated to covering a wide range of topics central to the study of cell and molecular biology, genetics, and other life-science fields. Through innovative print articles, online stories, and multimedia features, the magazine explores the latest scientific discoveries, trends in research, innovative techniques, new technology, business, and careers. It is read by leading researchers in industry and academia who value penetrating analyses and broad perspectives on life-science topics both within and beyond their areas of expertise. Written by prominent scientists and professional journalists, articles in are concise, accurate, accessible, and entertaining. View Full Profile Learn about our Editorial Policies . Dec 30, 2025 | 3 min read Save for Later Inspired by biomimicry, this postdoc investigates how material structure governs energy conversion efficiency. Image credit: University of Exeter, ©iStock.com, Petmal PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. My name is Anurag Roy . I am a research fellow at the University of Exeter in the Renewable Engineering department. As a material chemist, I focus on energy harvesting materials, studying their structure-property-performance relationships. I completed my PhD in 2019 at CSIR-CGCRI and Jadavpur University, before continuing my research here. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? I’ve always been curious about how things work, even as a kid. I remember taking apart gadgets and wondering why certain materials behaved the way they did. This early fascination grew into a deeper interest in science, especially when I learned about biomimicry—the idea of looking to nature for solutions. Nature’s designs are incredibly efficient, and I found it amazing how every material in the natural world has unique properties perfectly suited to its purpose. When I started studying renewable energy, I realized how important these natural principles could be in solving real-world problems. I became passionate about energy harvesting materials, intrigued by how their structure directly impacts their performance. Each material has its own story, its own strengths and limitations, and understanding these details felt like unlocking a puzzle. What truly motivates me is the idea of energy justice—making clean energy accessible and fair for everyone. It’s not just about creating better materials but about using science to help build a sustainable future where energy is available to all, regardless of background or location. This blend of curiosity, nature-inspired design, and social impact has shaped my research journey and continues to drive me every day. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research projects focuses on developing smart hydrogel membranes for smart windows. These materials are designed to respond to environmental changes, such as temperature and sunlight, by altering their transparency and thermal properties. The goal is to create windows that can automatically regulate heat and light entering a building, which can lead to significant energy savings. Traditional windows let in heat during summer and lose it during winter, which means buildings need a lot of energy for cooling and heating. By using these smart hydrogel membranes, windows can block or allow heat and light as needed, reducing the need for air conditioning and heating. This not only lowers energy bills but also decreases carbon emissions, helping combat climate change. What makes this project exciting is the use of biomimetic materials—materials inspired by nature. These hydrogels mimic how some natural systems adapt to their environment, making them efficient and sustainable. The ability to fine-tune the material’s structure allows us to optimize its performance, making the windows both smart and eco-friendly. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career/journey so far? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been seeing how fundamental research can lead to real-world impact. Early on, I focused on understanding the basic properties of materials, but over time, I’ve applied that knowledge to develop smart, sustainable solutions like energy-harvesting materials and smart window hydrogels. A big part of this process is data analysis and interpretation. Collecting data from experiments and making sense of it to understand how material structure affects performance is both challenging and rewarding. It’s like solving a puzzle where every detail matters. This deep dive into data helps me optimize materials for better energy efficiency and sustainability. Collaborating with researchers from diverse backgrounds has also been a highlight. It pushes me to think differently and improves the quality of my work. Moving from my PhD in India to a research role in the UK has broadened my perspective on global energy challenges, especially energy justice. Knowing my work could help make clean energy more accessible keeps me motivated every day. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Mitochondria are critical for neuronal energy demands and synaptic remodeling, yet little is known about how circRNAs influence mitochondrial behavior in neurons . The possibility that a stable, brain-enriched circRNA could directly modulate mitochondrial dynamics and neuronal plasticity opened an exciting and largely unexplored area of research. I am motivated by the opportunity to uncover new biological mechanisms that could ultimately contribute to therapies or biomarkers for neurological disorders. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve? CircRNAs are highly stable and abundant in neurons, yet the biological functions of most brain-enriched circRNAs remain unknown. Neurons rely heavily on precise mitochondrial transport, morphology, and bioenergetic balance to support synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. However, the molecular mechanisms linking non-coding RNAs to mitochondrial behavior in neurons are poorly understood. My research focuses on understanding how nuclear encoded mitochondrial circRNAs regulate neuronal and synaptic function through control of mitochondrial dynamics and local energy homeostasis. I aim to solve the fundamental problem of how circRNA contributes to neuronal health and plasticity, and whether their deregulation leads to synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration. Using neuronal models, biochemical assays, live-cell imaging, and functional studies, I investigate how circRNA influences mitochondrial stability, trafficking, and activity-dependent remodeling at synapses. This work could uncover a previously unrecognized layer of RNA-mediated mitochondrial regulation in the nervous system and identify circRNA as a potential therapeutic target or biomarker for neurological disorders associated with mitochondrial dysfunction. Target-Based Drug Discovery for Neurodegeneration Q | What have you learned from studying circRNA that you didn’t expect? One of the most unexpected things I learned from studying circRNA was how strongly mitochondrial behavior is linked to rapid synaptic remodeling in neurons. I initially approached circRNAs as gene regulatory molecules that would primarily influence transcriptional or post-transcriptional pathways. However, during my experiments, it became clear that altering circRNA levels could quickly affect mitochondrial stability, distribution, and dynamics within dendrites, ultimately influencing spine plasticity. This revealed a much more immediate and functional connection between non-coding RNAs and neuronal energetics than I had anticipated. Another surprising insight was the versatility of circRNAs themselves. Traditionally viewed as relatively passive regulators or microRNA sponges, circRNAs may play far more active roles in coordinating organelle function and cellular responses to neuronal activity. This shifted the way I think about RNA biology—not as isolated layers of regulation, but as integrated components of cellular architecture and signaling. The project taught me the importance of exploring unconventional hypotheses, especially in fields where many fundamental mechanisms remain unknown. Q | If your research succeeds, what could it change for science or society? This work could fundamentally expand our understanding of how non-coding RNAs regulate neuronal function and mitochondrial biology. While circRNAs are abundant in the brain, their physiological roles remain largely unexplored. Demonstrating that a circRNA actively controls mitochondrial dynamics and synaptic plasticity would uncover a new layer of gene regulation that links RNA biology directly to neuronal energy homeostasis and brain function. This could have important implications for diseases where mitochondrial dysfunction and synaptic degeneration are central features, including Alzheimer’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis , Parkinson’s disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders. By identifying circRNA-mediated pathways, the research may reveal novel therapeutic targets that can stabilize neuronal health or improve synaptic resilience. In the long term, circRNAs could also serve as minimally invasive biomarkers because of their remarkable stability in biological fluids. More broadly, this work could shift how scientists think about “non-coding” regions of the genome, highlighting that these RNAs are not passive byproducts, but active regulators of cellular organization, communication, and adaptation in the nervous system. Read More Q | What question are you most excited to answer next? I want to know how circRNA mechanistically communicates with mitochondria to regulate synaptic plasticity. While we are beginning to observe clear effects on mitochondrial stability and neuronal remodeling, the precise molecular pathway remains unknown. I am particularly interested in determining whether this circRNA acts through specific RNA-binding proteins, microRNA networks, or direct interactions with mitochondrial transport and dynamics machinery such as kinesins and fission-fusion regulators. I am also excited to explore whether the circRNA functions as an activity-dependent regulator during learning-like neuronal stimulation and whether its dysregulation contributes to neurodegenerative disease progression. Another important direction is investigating whether mitochondrial-associated circRNAs can be detected in extracellular vesicles or blood, potentially serving as biomarkers for neurological disorders. Answering these questions could bridge several fields—RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and neuroscience—and help define a broader principle of how non-coding RNAs coordinate cellular energetics with synaptic adaptation. The possibility of uncovering an entirely new regulatory mechanism is what motivates me most. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Dec 30, 2025 | 3 min read Save for Later Inspired by biomimicry, this postdoc investigates how material structure governs energy conversion efficiency. Image credit: University of Exeter, ©iStock.com, Petmal PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. My name is Anurag Roy . I am a research fellow at the University of Exeter in the Renewable Engineering department. As a material chemist, I focus on energy harvesting materials, studying their structure-property-performance relationships. I completed my PhD in 2019 at CSIR-CGCRI and Jadavpur University, before continuing my research here. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? I’ve always been curious about how things work, even as a kid. I remember taking apart gadgets and wondering why certain materials behaved the way they did. This early fascination grew into a deeper interest in science, especially when I learned about biomimicry—the idea of looking to nature for solutions. Nature’s designs are incredibly efficient, and I found it amazing how every material in the natural world has unique properties perfectly suited to its purpose. When I started studying renewable energy, I realized how important these natural principles could be in solving real-world problems. I became passionate about energy harvesting materials, intrigued by how their structure directly impacts their performance. Each material has its own story, its own strengths and limitations, and understanding these details felt like unlocking a puzzle. What truly motivates me is the idea of energy justice—making clean energy accessible and fair for everyone. It’s not just about creating better materials but about using science to help build a sustainable future where energy is available to all, regardless of background or location. This blend of curiosity, nature-inspired design, and social impact has shaped my research journey and continues to drive me every day. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research projects focuses on developing smart hydrogel membranes for smart windows. These materials are designed to respond to environmental changes, such as temperature and sunlight, by altering their transparency and thermal properties. The goal is to create windows that can automatically regulate heat and light entering a building, which can lead to significant energy savings. Traditional windows let in heat during summer and lose it during winter, which means buildings need a lot of energy for cooling and heating. By using these smart hydrogel membranes, windows can block or allow heat and light as needed, reducing the need for air conditioning and heating. This not only lowers energy bills but also decreases carbon emissions, helping combat climate change. What makes this project exciting is the use of biomimetic materials—materials inspired by nature. These hydrogels mimic how some natural systems adapt to their environment, making them efficient and sustainable. The ability to fine-tune the material’s structure allows us to optimize its performance, making the windows both smart and eco-friendly. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career/journey so far? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been seeing how fundamental research can lead to real-world impact. Early on, I focused on understanding the basic properties of materials, but over time, I’ve applied that knowledge to develop smart, sustainable solutions like energy-harvesting materials and smart window hydrogels. A big part of this process is data analysis and interpretation. Collecting data from experiments and making sense of it to understand how material structure affects performance is both challenging and rewarding. It’s like solving a puzzle where every detail matters. This deep dive into data helps me optimize materials for better energy efficiency and sustainability. Collaborating with researchers from diverse backgrounds has also been a highlight. It pushes me to think differently and improves the quality of my work. Moving from my PhD in India to a research role in the UK has broadened my perspective on global energy challenges, especially energy justice. Knowing my work could help make clean energy more accessible keeps me motivated every day. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series? Kaushik Chanda is a postdoctoral researcher at The Herbert Wertheim UF Scripps Institute for Biomedical Innovation & Technology (Scripps Research). He studies how long non-coding RNAs drive structural plasticity and mitochondrial homeostasis, with implications for preserving cognitive function in conditions like Alzheimer’s disease and Huntington’s disease. In this Postdoc Portrait interview, he shares how he hopes to bridge several fields of biology and reframe the regulatory role of these non-coding nucleic acids. Uncovering Non-coding RNA Functions in Synapses Q | What drew you to circular RNAs? I was drawn to the field of non-coding RNAs and neuronal biology by a fascination with how complex brain functions can be regulated by molecules once considered non-functional. During my research experience, I became particularly interested in the intersection of RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and synaptic plasticity because these processes are central to learning, memory, and neurodegenerative disease. Despite major advances in neuroscience, many mechanisms underlying neuronal adaptability and vulnerability remain unresolved, especially the role of circular RNAs (circRNAs) in regulating cellular organelles and synaptic function. My work on circRNAs emerged from this curiosity. Mitochondria are critical for neuronal energy demands and synaptic remodeling, yet little is known about how circRNAs influence mitochondrial behavior in neurons . The possibility that a stable, brain-enriched circRNA could directly modulate mitochondrial dynamics and neuronal plasticity opened an exciting and largely unexplored area of research. I am motivated by the opportunity to uncover new biological mechanisms that could ultimately contribute to therapies or biomarkers for neurological disorders. Continue reading below... Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve? CircRNAs are highly stable and abundant in neurons, yet the biological functions of most brain-enriched circRNAs remain unknown. Neurons rely heavily on precise mitochondrial transport, morphology, and bioenergetic balance to support synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. However, the molecular mechanisms linking non-coding RNAs to mitochondrial behavior in neurons are poorly understood. My research focuses on understanding how nuclear encoded mitochondrial circRNAs regulate neuronal and synaptic function through control of mitochondrial dynamics and local energy homeostasis. I aim to solve the fundamental problem of how circRNA contributes to neuronal health and plasticity, and whether their deregulation leads to synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration. Using neuronal models, biochemical assays, live-cell imaging, and functional studies, I investigate how circRNA influences mitochondrial stability, trafficking, and activity-dependent remodeling at synapses. This work could uncover a previously unrecognized layer of RNA-mediated mitochondrial regulation in the nervous system and identify circRNA as a potential therapeutic target or biomarker for neurological disorders associated with mitochondrial dysfunction. Target-Based Drug Discovery for Neurodegeneration Q | What have you learned from studying circRNA that you didn’t expect? One of the most unexpected things I learned from studying circRNA was how strongly mitochondrial behavior is linked to rapid synaptic remodeling in neurons. I initially approached circRNAs as gene regulatory molecules that would primarily influence transcriptional or post-transcriptional pathways. However, during my experiments, it became clear that altering circRNA levels could quickly affect mitochondrial stability, distribution, and dynamics within dendrites, ultimately influencing spine plasticity. This revealed a much more immediate and functional connection between non-coding RNAs and neuronal energetics than I had anticipated. Another surprising insight was the versatility of circRNAs themselves. Traditionally viewed as relatively passive regulators or microRNA sponges, circRNAs may play far more active roles in coordinating organelle function and cellular responses to neuronal activity. This shifted the way I think about RNA biology—not as isolated layers of regulation, but as integrated components of cellular architecture and signaling. The project taught me the importance of exploring unconventional hypotheses, especially in fields where many fundamental mechanisms remain unknown. Q | If your research succeeds, what could it change for science or society? This work could fundamentally expand our understanding of how non-coding RNAs regulate neuronal function and mitochondrial biology. While circRNAs are abundant in the brain, their physiological roles remain largely unexplored. Demonstrating that a circRNA actively controls mitochondrial dynamics and synaptic plasticity would uncover a new layer of gene regulation that links RNA biology directly to neuronal energy homeostasis and brain function. This could have important implications for diseases where mitochondrial dysfunction and synaptic degeneration are central features, including Alzheimer’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis , Parkinson’s disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders. By identifying circRNA-mediated pathways, the research may reveal novel therapeutic targets that can stabilize neuronal health or improve synaptic resilience. In the long term, circRNAs could also serve as minimally invasive biomarkers because of their remarkable stability in biological fluids. More broadly, this work could shift how scientists think about “non-coding” regions of the genome, highlighting that these RNAs are not passive byproducts, but active regulators of cellular organization, communication, and adaptation in the nervous system. Read More Q | What question are you most excited to answer next? I want to know how circRNA mechanistically communicates with mitochondria to regulate synaptic plasticity. While we are beginning to observe clear effects on mitochondrial stability and neuronal remodeling, the precise molecular pathway remains unknown. I am particularly interested in determining whether this circRNA acts through specific RNA-binding proteins, microRNA networks, or direct interactions with mitochondrial transport and dynamics machinery such as kinesins and fission-fusion regulators. I am also excited to explore whether the circRNA functions as an activity-dependent regulator during learning-like neuronal stimulation and whether its dysregulation contributes to neurodegenerative disease progression. Another important direction is investigating whether mitochondrial-associated circRNAs can be detected in extracellular vesicles or blood, potentially serving as biomarkers for neurological disorders. Answering these questions could bridge several fields—RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and neuroscience—and help define a broader principle of how non-coding RNAs coordinate cellular energetics with synaptic adaptation. The possibility of uncovering an entirely new regulatory mechanism is what motivates me most. Responses have been edited for length and clarity. Uncovering Non-coding RNA Functions in Synapses
Q | What drew you to circular RNAs?
Q |
I was drawn to the field of non-coding RNAs and neuronal biology by a fascination with how complex brain functions can be regulated by molecules once considered non-functional. During my research experience, I became particularly interested in the intersection of RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and synaptic plasticity because these processes are central to learning, memory, and neurodegenerative disease. Despite major advances in neuroscience, many mechanisms underlying neuronal adaptability and vulnerability remain unresolved, especially the role of circular RNAs (circRNAs) in regulating cellular organelles and synaptic function.
My work on circRNAs emerged from this curiosity. Mitochondria are critical for neuronal energy demands and synaptic remodeling, yet little is known about how circRNAs influence mitochondrial behavior in neurons . The possibility that a stable, brain-enriched circRNA could directly modulate mitochondrial dynamics and neuronal plasticity opened an exciting and largely unexplored area of research. I am motivated by the opportunity to uncover new biological mechanisms that could ultimately contribute to therapies or biomarkers for neurological disorders.

Q | What scientific problem are you trying to solve?
CircRNAs are highly stable and abundant in neurons, yet the biological functions of most brain-enriched circRNAs remain unknown. Neurons rely heavily on precise mitochondrial transport, morphology, and bioenergetic balance to support synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. However, the molecular mechanisms linking non-coding RNAs to mitochondrial behavior in neurons are poorly understood. My research focuses on understanding how nuclear encoded mitochondrial circRNAs regulate neuronal and synaptic function through control of mitochondrial dynamics and local energy homeostasis.

I aim to solve the fundamental problem of how circRNA contributes to neuronal health and plasticity, and whether their deregulation leads to synaptic dysfunction and neurodegeneration. Using neuronal models, biochemical assays, live-cell imaging, and functional studies, I investigate how circRNA influences mitochondrial stability, trafficking, and activity-dependent remodeling at synapses. This work could uncover a previously unrecognized layer of RNA-mediated mitochondrial regulation in the nervous system and identify circRNA as a potential therapeutic target or biomarker for neurological disorders associated with mitochondrial dysfunction.

Q | What have you learned from studying circRNA that you didn’t expect?
One of the most unexpected things I learned from studying circRNA was how strongly mitochondrial behavior is linked to rapid synaptic remodeling in neurons. I initially approached circRNAs as gene regulatory molecules that would primarily influence transcriptional or post-transcriptional pathways. However, during my experiments, it became clear that altering circRNA levels could quickly affect mitochondrial stability, distribution, and dynamics within dendrites, ultimately influencing spine plasticity. This revealed a much more immediate and functional connection between non-coding RNAs and neuronal energetics than I had anticipated.
Another surprising insight was the versatility of circRNAs themselves. Traditionally viewed as relatively passive regulators or microRNA sponges, circRNAs may play far more active roles in coordinating organelle function and cellular responses to neuronal activity. This shifted the way I think about RNA biology—not as isolated layers of regulation, but as integrated components of cellular architecture and signaling. The project taught me the importance of exploring unconventional hypotheses, especially in fields where many fundamental mechanisms remain unknown.
Q | If your research succeeds, what could it change for science or society?
This work could fundamentally expand our understanding of how non-coding RNAs regulate neuronal function and mitochondrial biology. While circRNAs are abundant in the brain, their physiological roles remain largely unexplored. Demonstrating that a circRNA actively controls mitochondrial dynamics and synaptic plasticity would uncover a new layer of gene regulation that links RNA biology directly to neuronal energy homeostasis and brain function. This could have important implications for diseases where mitochondrial dysfunction and synaptic degeneration are central features, including Alzheimer’s disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis , Parkinson’s disease, and other neurodegenerative disorders. By identifying circRNA-mediated pathways, the research may reveal novel therapeutic targets that can stabilize neuronal health or improve synaptic resilience. In the long term, circRNAs could also serve as minimally invasive biomarkers because of their remarkable stability in biological fluids. More broadly, this work could shift how scientists think about “non-coding” regions of the genome, highlighting that these RNAs are not passive byproducts, but active regulators of cellular organization, communication, and adaptation in the nervous system.

Q | What question are you most excited to answer next?
I want to know how circRNA mechanistically communicates with mitochondria to regulate synaptic plasticity. While we are beginning to observe clear effects on mitochondrial stability and neuronal remodeling, the precise molecular pathway remains unknown. I am particularly interested in determining whether this circRNA acts through specific RNA-binding proteins, microRNA networks, or direct interactions with mitochondrial transport and dynamics machinery such as kinesins and fission-fusion regulators. I am also excited to explore whether the circRNA functions as an activity-dependent regulator during learning-like neuronal stimulation and whether its dysregulation contributes to neurodegenerative disease progression. Another important direction is investigating whether mitochondrial-associated circRNAs can be detected in extracellular vesicles or blood, potentially serving as biomarkers for neurological disorders. Answering these questions could bridge several fields—RNA biology, mitochondrial dynamics, and neuroscience—and help define a broader principle of how non-coding RNAs coordinate cellular energetics with synaptic adaptation. The possibility of uncovering an entirely new regulatory mechanism is what motivates me most.

Dec 30, 2025 | 3 min read Save for Later Inspired by biomimicry, this postdoc investigates how material structure governs energy conversion efficiency. Image credit: University of Exeter, ©iStock.com, Petmal PDF version Share Q | Write a brief introduction to yourself including the lab you work in and your research background. My name is Anurag Roy . I am a research fellow at the University of Exeter in the Renewable Engineering department. As a material chemist, I focus on energy harvesting materials, studying their structure-property-performance relationships. I completed my PhD in 2019 at CSIR-CGCRI and Jadavpur University, before continuing my research here. Q | How did you first get interested in science and/or your field of research? I’ve always been curious about how things work, even as a kid. I remember taking apart gadgets and wondering why certain materials behaved the way they did. This early fascination grew into a deeper interest in science, especially when I learned about biomimicry—the idea of looking to nature for solutions. Nature’s designs are incredibly efficient, and I found it amazing how every material in the natural world has unique properties perfectly suited to its purpose. When I started studying renewable energy, I realized how important these natural principles could be in solving real-world problems. I became passionate about energy harvesting materials, intrigued by how their structure directly impacts their performance. Each material has its own story, its own strengths and limitations, and understanding these details felt like unlocking a puzzle. What truly motivates me is the idea of energy justice—making clean energy accessible and fair for everyone. It’s not just about creating better materials but about using science to help build a sustainable future where energy is available to all, regardless of background or location. This blend of curiosity, nature-inspired design, and social impact has shaped my research journey and continues to drive me every day. Continue reading below... Like this story? Sign up for FREE Cell Biology updates: Latest science news stories Topic-tailored resources and events Customized newsletter content Subscribe Q | Tell us about your favorite research project you’re working on. One of my favorite research projects focuses on developing smart hydrogel membranes for smart windows. These materials are designed to respond to environmental changes, such as temperature and sunlight, by altering their transparency and thermal properties. The goal is to create windows that can automatically regulate heat and light entering a building, which can lead to significant energy savings. Traditional windows let in heat during summer and lose it during winter, which means buildings need a lot of energy for cooling and heating. By using these smart hydrogel membranes, windows can block or allow heat and light as needed, reducing the need for air conditioning and heating. This not only lowers energy bills but also decreases carbon emissions, helping combat climate change. What makes this project exciting is the use of biomimetic materials—materials inspired by nature. These hydrogels mimic how some natural systems adapt to their environment, making them efficient and sustainable. The ability to fine-tune the material’s structure allows us to optimize its performance, making the windows both smart and eco-friendly. Q | What has been the most exciting part of your scientific career/journey so far? The most exciting part of my scientific journey has been seeing how fundamental research can lead to real-world impact. Early on, I focused on understanding the basic properties of materials, but over time, I’ve applied that knowledge to develop smart, sustainable solutions like energy-harvesting materials and smart window hydrogels. A big part of this process is data analysis and interpretation. Collecting data from experiments and making sense of it to understand how material structure affects performance is both challenging and rewarding. It’s like solving a puzzle where every detail matters. This deep dive into data helps me optimize materials for better energy efficiency and sustainability. Collaborating with researchers from diverse backgrounds has also been a highlight. It pushes me to think differently and improves the quality of my work. Moving from my PhD in India to a research role in the UK has broadened my perspective on global energy challenges, especially energy justice. Knowing my work could help make clean energy more accessible keeps me motivated every day. Are you a researcher who would like to be featured in the “Postdoc Portraits” series?
